source_paper_title: Asymptotically Optimal Control of Make-to-Stock Systems
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Gao_2023, title={Asymptotically Optimal Control of Make-to-Stock Systems}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.161}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.161}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Gao, Xuefeng and Huang, Junfei}, year={2023}, month=june }
context:
Section 1 (step 2 discussion) and Conclusion/Section 9, pages 3–4 and 54

Developing efficient algorithms for multi-stage SPs is an active area of research. Although we solve the SP for some simple examples in Section 8, solving more general cases is well beyond the scope of this paper...

How to extend this strategy to multistage SPs is an issue yet to be explored.
title: Computationally efficient methods for solving multistage stochastic programs in ATO inventory control
background_latex:
Consider a continuous-review assemble-to-order (ATO) inventory system with:
1) A set of products indexed by \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\) and components indexed by \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\).
2) A bill-of-materials matrix \(A\in\mathbb{Z}_+^{n\times m}\) where \(a_{ji}\) units of component \(j\) are required per unit of product \(i\).
3) Deterministic component lead times taking \(K\) distinct values \(0<L_1<\cdots<L_K\). Components are grouped by lead time: \(A^k\) denotes the submatrix of \(A\) consisting of the rows (components) whose lead time equals \(L_k\), and \(h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^{n_k}\) are their holding costs.
4) Backlog costs \(b\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\). Define \(c=b+\sum_{k=1}^K (A^k)^\top h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\).
5) Demand over time is modeled via random vectors \(D^k\in\mathbb{Z}_+^m\) (\(k=1,\dots,K\)) representing demand increments over disjoint lead-time-related intervals. (Only their distributions matter for the stochastic program below.)

A lower bound on the long-run average cost is given by the optimal value \(\varphi_K\) of a \((K+1)\)-stage stochastic program:
\[
\varphi_K=\inf_{y_K\in\mathbb{R}^{n_K}}\Bigl\{h^K\cdot y_K+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{K-1}(y_K,D^K)\bigr]\Bigr\},
\]
\[
\varphi_k(y_{k+1},\dots,y_K,x)=\inf_{y_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_k}}\Bigl\{h^k\cdot y_k+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{k-1}(y_k,\dots,y_K,x+D^k)\bigr]\Bigr\},\quad k=1,\dots,K-1,
\]
and the last-stage linear program
\[
\varphi_0(y_1,\dots,y_K,x)=-\max_{z\in\mathbb{R}^m}\Bigl\{c\cdot z\ \big|\ z\le x,\ A^k z\le y_k\ (k=1,\dots,K)\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m\) represents accumulated demand over the entire longest lead-time window, and the decisions \(y_k\) represent (relaxed) component-availability quantities at each stage.

The paper emphasizes that to implement their asymptotically optimal policy, one must repeatedly (re-)solve such multistage stochastic programs (or high-dimensional LP approximations of them) as system state changes. However, scenario-tree formulations can have extremely high dimension when \(K\) is large and/or lead times are long, making direct solution computationally burdensome.
problem_definition_latex: Design algorithmic methods that, for general \(K\), general bill-of-materials matrix \(A\), and general (given) demand distributions of \((D^1,\dots,D^K)\), can solve (exactly or to a controllable accuracy) the multistage stochastic program defining \(\varphi_K\) above with substantially reduced computational complexity relative to the naive scenario-tree linear-program formulation, in a way suitable for repeated re-optimization over time within an ATO control policy.
latex:
Consider a continuous-review assemble-to-order (ATO) inventory system with:
1) A set of products indexed by \(i\in\{1,\dots,m\}\) and components indexed by \(j\in\{1,\dots,n\}\).
2) A bill-of-materials matrix \(A\in\mathbb{Z}_+^{n\times m}\) where \(a_{ji}\) units of component \(j\) are required per unit of product \(i\).
3) Deterministic component lead times taking \(K\) distinct values \(0<L_1<\cdots<L_K\). Components are grouped by lead time: \(A^k\) denotes the submatrix of \(A\) consisting of the rows (components) whose lead time equals \(L_k\), and \(h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^{n_k}\) are their holding costs.
4) Backlog costs \(b\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\). Define \(c=b+\sum_{k=1}^K (A^k)^\top h^k\in\mathbb{R}_{++}^m\).
5) Demand over time is modeled via random vectors \(D^k\in\mathbb{Z}_+^m\) (\(k=1,\dots,K\)) representing demand increments over disjoint lead-time-related intervals. (Only their distributions matter for the stochastic program below.)

A lower bound on the long-run average cost is given by the optimal value \(\varphi_K\) of a \((K+1)\)-stage stochastic program:
\[
\varphi_K=\inf_{y_K\in\mathbb{R}^{n_K}}\Bigl\{h^K\cdot y_K+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{K-1}(y_K,D^K)\bigr]\Bigr\},
\]
\[
\varphi_k(y_{k+1},\dots,y_K,x)=\inf_{y_k\in\mathbb{R}^{n_k}}\Bigl\{h^k\cdot y_k+\mathbb{E}\bigl[\varphi_{k-1}(y_k,\dots,y_K,x+D^k)\bigr]\Bigr\},\quad k=1,\dots,K-1,
\]
and the last-stage linear program
\[
\varphi_0(y_1,\dots,y_K,x)=-\max_{z\in\mathbb{R}^m}\Bigl\{c\cdot z\ \big|\ z\le x,\ A^k z\le y_k\ (k=1,\dots,K)\Bigr\}.
\]
Here \(x\in\mathbb{R}^m\) represents accumulated demand over the entire longest lead-time window, and the decisions \(y_k\) represent (relaxed) component-availability quantities at each stage.

The paper emphasizes that to implement their asymptotically optimal policy, one must repeatedly (re-)solve such multistage stochastic programs (or high-dimensional LP approximations of them) as system state changes. However, scenario-tree formulations can have extremely high dimension when \(K\) is large and/or lead times are long, making direct solution computationally burdensome.

Design algorithmic methods that, for general \(K\), general bill-of-materials matrix \(A\), and general (given) demand distributions of \((D^1,\dots,D^K)\), can solve (exactly or to a controllable accuracy) the multistage stochastic program defining \(\varphi_K\) above with substantially reduced computational complexity relative to the naive scenario-tree linear-program formulation, in a way suitable for repeated re-optimization over time within an ATO control policy.
location_in_paper: Section 1 (step 2 discussion) and Conclusion/Section 9, pages 3–4 and 54
open_problem_quote:
Developing efficient algorithms for multi-stage SPs is an active area of research. Although we solve the SP for some simple examples in Section 8, solving more general cases is well beyond the scope of this paper...

How to extend this strategy to multistage SPs is an issue yet to be explored.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: This is an algorithm-design problem rather than a conjectured inequality or structural claim; there is no clear finite instance space where a single counterexample would resolve the question.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate algorithms (decomposition, approximations, rolling-horizon schemes) can be represented as code and evaluated on simulated ATO instances by runtime/accuracy trade-offs, but the objective is not crisply specified as a single computable fitness across all instances, and theoretical guarantees would still be needed.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The multistage stochastic program \(\varphi_K\) in Reiman--Wan--Wang (2021) is a diffusion-scale lower bound and a parameter generator for their asymptotically optimal ATO control. The open computational issue is that naive scenario-tree LP formulations of \(\varphi_K\) scale poorly with the number of stages \(K\), lead-time lengths, and demand support. Among the forward citations provided, the most directly relevant work is the paper on asymptotically optimal energy consumption and inventory control in a make-to-stock system, which contributes technical ingredients toward computation: it shows an equivalent SP formulation with finite optimizers, proposes demand-truncation leading to finite-dimensional LP approximations, proves uniform convergence of the approximations to the original value functions, and establishes Lipschitz/stability of the unique optimal solution with constants independent of truncation size. These results support repeated re-optimization by ensuring that approximate solutions behave regularly as inputs change.

However, the truncation approach still relies on scenario-tree LPs and does not by itself yield a fundamentally reduced-complexity algorithm for general \(K\) and general BOM matrices \(A\). The other citing paper provided is an online-learning result for a different single-item lost-sales model and does not contribute to solving \(\varphi_K\). Based on the limited forward-citation set supplied here, the problem of designing substantially more computationally efficient general-purpose solvers for the \((K+1)\)-stage ATO stochastic program remains open; promising directions suggested by the source paper include exploiting BOM-induced structure for decomposition/rounding and reducing effective stage count when lead-time differences are small fractions of the longest lead time.

sources.title: Asymptotically optimal energy consumption and inventory control in a make-to-stock manufacturing system
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper reformulates the multistage stochastic program lower bound for general deterministic lead times into an equivalent SP with finite optimal solutions (their (13)), proves boundedness of optimal stage decisions (Theorem 1) and equality of the lower bound value (Theorem 2: \(\bar C=\varphi_K + b\cdot \mathbb E[\bar D]\)). For computation/stability, it proposes a finite-dimensional LP approximation by truncating demands \(D^k\) to \(D_M^k=D^k\wedge M\) (SP (92)) and shows uniform convergence \(\varphi_{k,M}\to \varphi_k\) as \(M\to\infty\) (Theorem 5). It further proves that the unique optimal solution of the approximating LP is Lipschitz in inputs with a constant independent of the scenario-tree size \(M\) (Theorem 6: \(\|y^*_{k,M,a}-y^*_{k,M,b}\|_\infty\le \kappa(\|x_a-x_b\|_\infty+\sum_{\ell=k+1}^K\|y^\ell_a-y^\ell_b\|_\infty)\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computationally efficient methods to solve the general \((K+1)\)-stage SP defining \(\varphi_K\) with reduced complexity vs naive scenario trees, suitable for repeated re-optimization. This paper does not provide a new fast algorithm that avoids scenario-tree growth; in fact its LP approximation (93) is essentially a scenario-tree LP for truncated demands. However, it makes partial progress by (i) giving an equivalent SP (13) whose optimizers are finite (enabling repeated re-optimization conceptually), (ii) providing a controllable-accuracy approximation scheme via truncation \(M\) with uniform error control (Theorem 5), and (iii) establishing strong stability/Lipschitz properties of the approximating LP’s unique optimizer with constants independent of problem size (Theorem 6), which is a key ingredient for designing iterative/online methods and for justifying less frequent re-solves. The paper itself explicitly states that developing computationally efficient solvers for general multistage SPs is beyond its scope.

sources.title: Blessing of Order Crossover for Inventory Systems
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper studies a single-item periodic-review lost-sales inventory system with lead time \(L\) and uncertain supply \(s(q,Z)\), with unknown i.i.d. demand \(D_t\) and supply noise \(Z_t\). It proposes an online learning algorithm (Algorithm 1) that learns an approximately optimal constant-order quantity \(q^*\) from censored observations and proves a finite-horizon regret bound (Theorem 1): for \(K=\sqrt{T}\) grid points and \(\gamma_n=2^{-n}\), \(\mathrm{Regret}_T\le \kappa\,\kappa_2\,(L+\sqrt{T})\log T\), where regret is measured against the optimal constant-order policy’s long-run average cost.
sources.connection: The open problem asks for computationally efficient methods to solve a \((K+1)\)-stage stochastic program \(\varphi_K\) arising in multi-component, multi-product assemble-to-order (ATO) systems with multiple distinct lead times and general bill-of-materials matrix \(A\), suitable for repeated re-optimization. This citing paper does not address multistage stochastic programming for ATO, nor does it propose algorithms for evaluating/optimizing \(\varphi_K\) or its scenario-tree LP. Instead it develops an online learning/regret-minimization method for a different model class: single-product lost-sales with uncertain supply, focusing on learning a constant-order heuristic. While it shares themes of lead times, curse of dimensionality, and computational intractability of optimal control, it does not provide algorithms to compute the multistage value functions \(\varphi_k\) or to reduce scenario-tree complexity in the ATO stochastic program.
